evidence_strength: committed executable contract; runtime result absent

sources.finding: Dead PID lock recovery renames stale lock directories into unique quarantine paths and removes them before retrying acquisition.
Existing tests cover one process death at each write boundary but do not test repeated cycles or residual artifact accumulation.

finding: Single-cycle crash recovery is insufficient to establish operational boundedness. Repeated process deaths must preserve exactly one terminal while leaving no .lock, .stale.*, or .tmp coordination artifacts after each successor recovery.
design_implication: Treat cleanup boundedness as a separate durability invariant from terminal correctness. A runtime is acceptable only when both the journal state and coordination filesystem return to a clean state after every crash/restart cycle.

assertions: successor records or observes exactly one terminal
no .lock, .stale.*, or .tmp artifacts remain after each cycle

verification.source_fetch: committed file fetched successfully from commit f24265461e6e6041282bb952f70c7a37727f0f56

uncertainty: The contract has not yet run on a clean Linux or macOS runner.
PID reuse and platform-specific directory sync semantics remain outside this test.
Twelve cycles detect accumulation regressions but are not a long-duration endurance benchmark.

failure_conditions: wrong terminal survives relative to crash boundary
test process hangs or exits nonzero
